name: BPC-157
headline: Recovery-focused peptide often explored for soft tissue and gut support.
summary: BPC-157 is commonly researched in recovery-oriented protocols where people want a practical way to track tissue-support dosing, local use, and how it overlaps with broader healing stacks.
mechanism: Often described in research contexts as a repair-oriented signaling peptide associated with angiogenesis, tissue remodeling, and recovery support.
considerations: Double-check concentration carefully because doses are often measured in micrograms.
Frequent dosing can make duplicate same-day logs easy to miss.
Local injection plans should still stay consistent with total daily intake.
overlapSignals: Often paired with TB-500 in repair-focused stacks.
Overlapping recovery compounds should surface duplicate intent, not just duplicate names.
sideEffectSignals: Unexpected dose escalation from unit confusion

name: CJC-1295
headline: Longer-acting growth-hormone secretagogue used in structured schedules.
summary: CJC-1295 is a good example of why the library should translate research-oriented compounds into plain-language protocol guidance around cadence, overlap, and longer persistence.
mechanism: Typically described as a GHRH analog used to support growth hormone signaling over an extended duration, especially in DAC form.
considerations: Users need to know which form they are using because cadence expectations differ.
Longer-acting compounds should surface a clearer washout and overlap story.
It should be obvious when this is being paired with another GH-support peptide.
overlapSignals: Often combined with Ipamorelin or other secretagogues.
Growth-stack overlap should be visible at both library and logging levels.
sideEffectSignals: Schedule confusion between DAC and non-DAC variants

name: Ipamorelin
headline: Shorter-acting secretagogue where easy logging matters more than complexity.
summary: Ipamorelin belongs in a user-friendly library because it is the kind of compound that creates friction fast if the app does not simplify frequent logging and stack context.
mechanism: Often described as a ghrelin receptor agonist or GH secretagogue associated with shorter-acting pulsatile support.
considerations: Frequent use increases the risk of accidental duplicate logs in the same day.
Users should be able to reuse a saved vial and one-tap a recurring dose.
Schedule clarity matters more here than a dense wall of education.
overlapSignals: Should flag overlap with CJC-1295 and other GH-support stacks.
Frequent-use compounds should surface recent history right in the log action.

name: PT-141
headline: As-needed peptide where recency and duplicate-dose warnings matter most.
summary: PT-141 is exactly the sort of compound where the library should explain what it is in plain language while the rest of the app focuses on timing confidence and last-dose visibility.
mechanism: Typically discussed as a melanocortin receptor agonist affecting central arousal pathways rather than vascular pathways.
considerations: As-needed compounds still need strong duplicate-dose protection.
Last taken time should be very visible in the UI.
Users may care more about onset, recency, and side effects than ongoing cycle structure.
overlapSignals: Short recency windows should trigger warnings if another entry exists nearby.
As-needed use can hide accidental repeat dosing if the app is not explicit.

name: Semaglutide
headline: Metabolic peptide associated with appetite control and glucose regulation.
summary: Semaglutide belongs in the library as a high-clarity metabolic entry where users can quickly understand weekly cadence, appetite effects, and how it relates to glucose and weight trends.
mechanism: A GLP-1 receptor agonist discussed in clinical use for appetite suppression, slower gastric emptying, and glucose-dependent metabolic effects.
considerations: Slow escalation matters because appetite and GI effects can become the dominant user experience.
Weekly compounds need especially strong protection against accidental duplicate logs.
Users should be able to correlate it against weight, glucose, HRV, and GI notes.
overlapSignals: Multiple appetite- or glucose-focused compounds should trigger extra caution.
The app should highlight other active metabolic compounds when this is selected.

name: TB-500
headline: Longer-acting recovery peptide often tracked as part of injury-support stacks.
summary: TB-500 fits best in a library when the user needs simple explanations of cadence, stack overlap, and why a longer-acting recovery compound should not be re-dosed casually.
mechanism: Typically discussed as a thymosin beta-4 related peptide associated with cell migration, angiogenesis, and recovery signaling.
considerations: Longer half-life means overlap can build if weekly totals creep upward.
A clean protocol view should emphasize last dose and next safe window.
Users should be warned when stacking with another repair-class compound.
overlapSignals: Commonly stacked with BPC-157 in repair cycles.
Longer-acting recovery compounds deserve stronger duplicate-dose warnings.
